title: Multiplicative functions in short intervals II

thm:1.7.summary: Main theorem for complex multiplicative $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{U}$: for $\rho < \rho_1 := 1/3 - 2/(3\pi)$ and $2\le h\le X^{1/2}$, the short-interval average is approximated by $\frac1h\int_x^{x+h} u^{it_{f,X}}\,du$ times the long mean, for almost all $x$ — extending Matomäki–Radziwiłł I to complex-valued functions with an explicit pretentious main term.

thm:1.9.summary: Precise version for $(\alpha,X^\theta)$-non-vanishing multiplicative $f$: with the natural scale $h=h_0 H(f;X)$, an explicit short-interval approximation with error governed by $\frac{\log\log h_0}{\log h_0}$ and the mean-value functional $\widehat M(f;X)$.

cor:1.10.summary: Quantitative short-interval bound for non-vanishing $f$ obtained from Theorem 1.9, bounding $|\frac1h\sum_{x<n\le x+h}f(n)|$ by the long-average size plus explicit error terms.

thm:1.11.summary: Lower-bound / dense-set version: for non-negative $(\alpha,X^\theta)$-non-vanishing $f:\mathbb{N}\to[0,1]$, the short-interval sum is bounded below for all but few $x$ — the form behind density and Hooley-type applications.

thm:1.4.summary: Application to norm-forms: for a number field $K/\mathbb{Q}$, the non-negative norm-forms equidistribute in short intervals of length $h_0\delta_K(X)^{-1}$ with the expected constant $C_K$.

thm:1.5.summary: Almost-all-intervals version for norm-forms: short intervals of length $\to\infty$ (on the norm-form scale) contain the expected count of norm-forms of $K$ for almost all $x$.

cor:1.1.summary: Short-interval result for a general multiplicative subset $\mathcal{N}\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ satisfying a density hypothesis (2) with exponent $\alpha$: the abstract form from which the smooth-number and norm-form applications specialize.

cor:1.2.summary: Counting intervals where a multiplicative subset $\mathcal{N}$ is over/under-represented: an exceptional-set bound following from Corollary 1.1.

cor:1.3.summary: Smooth numbers in short intervals: the number of $(x,x+h]$ with no $x^\theta$-smooth number is $\ll_{\varepsilon,\theta} X h^{-1/2+\varepsilon}$; plus consecutive-smooth-number gap bounds.

def:1.6.summary: The pretentious mean-value functional $\widehat M(f;X) := \min_{|t|\le X}\sum_{p\le X}\frac{1-\Re f(p)p^{-it}}{p}$, measuring how far $f$ is from a twisted constant — the quantity governing the size of $f$'s averages.

def:1.8.summary: $(\alpha,\Delta)$-non-vanishing: a multiplicative $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{U}$ whose values avoid a degenerate pretentious configuration at scale $\Delta$ — the hypothesis under which the precise Theorems 1.9 and 1.11 hold.

lem:3.1.summary: Mean value theorem for Dirichlet polynomials with off-diagonal correction: $\int_{-T}^T|A(it)|^2\,dt \ll T\sum_{n\le N}|a_n|^2 + T\sum_n\sum_{0<|k|}\cdots$ — the $L^2$ baseline for sparse polynomials.

lem:3.4.summary: Mean value for short-support polynomials: for $A(s)=\sum_{x<n\le x+y}a_n n^{-s}$ with $x\ge y\ge x^\theta$, controls $\int|A|^2$ over the sparse Dirichlet polynomial.

lem:4.1.summary: Halász–Montgomery inequality for integers: the large-value bound $\sum_{t\in\mathcal{T}}|A(it)|^2$ for one-spaced $\mathcal{T}\subseteq[-T,T]$ — the sharp large-value estimate.

lem:4.2.summary: Refined large-value count for a length-$N$ Dirichlet polynomial in terms of $G=\sum_{N<n\le 2N}|a_n|^2 n^{-2}$, a variant of the Halász–Montgomery bound used for sparser configurations.

lem:4.3.summary: Sieve majorant set $S^+$: for $\mathcal{P}\subset\mathbb{P}\cap[1,z]$ with $P(z)=\prod_{p\in\mathcal{P}}p$, constructs a set $S^+$ with controlled sieve weights — the combinatorial input feeding the large-value estimates.

lem:4.4.summary: Halász–Montgomery inequality for primes: the prime-supported analogue of Lemma 4.1 for $P(s)=\sum_{N<p\le 2N}a(p)p^{it}$, the large-value input for the prime factor.

lem:6.1.summary: Decomposition of Dirichlet polynomials over factorization scales: for multiplicative $g:\mathbb{N}\to[0,1]$ and sequences with $a_{mp}=b_m c_p$ ($p\nmid m$, $P\le p\le Q$), splits the polynomial into product pieces.

lem:7.1.summary: Moment computation: high-power moment bound for the factored polynomial with $\ell=\lceil\log Y_2/\log Y_1\rceil$, controlling $\int|Q^\ell A|^2$ for multiplicative $g:\mathbb{N}\to[0,1]$.

lem:9.1.summary: Completely multiplicative models $g_{\mathcal{J}}$ for $\mathcal{J}\subseteq\{1,\dots,J+2\}$ used to split $f\cdot 1_{\mathcal{S}}$ scale-by-scale inside the factorization set.

lem:10.3.summary: Lower-bound sieve weights $\lambda_d=\mu(d)g^\star(d)\cdots$ for multiplicative $g$ with the cutoff $\chi$, realizing the well-factorable sieve that glues the $\mathcal{S}$-theorem to all integers.

lem:10.5.summary: Upper-bound sieve / Euler-product control: for non-negative multiplicative $f$, $\frac1X\sum_{X<n\le 2X}f(n) \le 10\prod_p(\cdots)$ — bounds the mean of $f$ by an explicit Euler product.

lem:13.1.summary: Number-field constants: for $K/\mathbb{Q}$ there exist $\rho(K),\alpha(K)$ and integers $M(K),R(K)$ describing the density and multiplicative structure of the norm-forms — the input to the norm-form applications.

prop:8.3.summary: Parseval bound for sparse Dirichlet polynomials: the $\int |F(1+it)|^2$ estimate over the factorization structure, built from the Halász–Montgomery mean and large-value theorems of §§3–4 — the analytic engine of the method.

thm:9.2.summary: Theorem inside the factorization set $\mathcal{S}$ ($\eta\in(0,1/6-\nu_2/3)$): the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted short-interval estimate from which the main theorems are deduced.

prop:10.4.summary: Sieve estimate bounding the exceptional set $\mathcal{E}(h)$ where the factorization structure fails, for $1\le P\le Q\le X^{3/4}$ — the sieve input gluing the $\mathcal{S}$-theorem back to all integers.

prop:12.1.summary: Lower-bound proposition for non-vanishing $f$ via a prime-factor decomposition $\mathcal{P}=\mathbb{P}\cap(X^\varepsilon,\cdot]$, the engine behind the dense/lower-bound Theorem 1.11.

edges.note: The $\mathcal{S}$-restricted theorem is derived from the Parseval bound (Proposition 8.3).
Section 11 deduces the main complex-multiplicative theorem from the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted Theorem 9.2.
The precise non-vanishing version is also obtained from the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted theorem.
Passing from the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted theorem to all integers uses the sieve estimate (Proposition 10.4).
The quantitative short-interval bound follows from Theorem 1.9.
Theorem 1.9 assumes $(\alpha,X^\theta)$-non-vanishing (Definition 1.8).
The error term of Theorem 1.9 is phrased via the mean-value functional $\widehat M(f;X)$ (Definition 1.6).
The lower-bound/dense theorem is built on the lower-bound proposition (12.1).
The dense theorem assumes a non-negative $(\alpha,X^\theta)$-non-vanishing $f$ (Definition 1.8).
The lower-bound proposition uses the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted theorem.
The lower-bound argument uses the upper-bound sieve / Euler-product control (Lemma 10.5).
The general multiplicative-subset result specializes the main theorem 1.7.
The over/under-representation interval count follows from Corollary 1.1.
The smooth-numbers-in-short-intervals bound follows from Corollary 1.2.
Equidistribution of norm-forms in short intervals specializes the multiplicative-subset Corollary 1.1.
The norm-form application uses the number-field constants (Lemma 13.1).
The almost-all-intervals norm-form result follows from Theorem 1.4.
The Parseval bound rests on the Halász–Montgomery inequality for integers (Lemma 4.1).
The prime-factor part uses the Halász–Montgomery inequality for primes (Lemma 4.4).
The high-moment contribution is controlled by the moment computation (Lemma 7.1).
The size of $|F(1+it)|$ is measured by the mean-value functional (Definition 1.6).
The Halász–Montgomery inequality builds on the mean value theorem (Lemma 3.1).
The refined large-value count also derives from the mean value theorem (Lemma 3.1).
The prime Halász–Montgomery inequality specializes the integer version (Lemma 4.1).
The prime large-value bound uses the sieve majorant set $S^+$ (Lemma 4.3).
The moment computation uses the Dirichlet-polynomial decomposition (Lemma 6.1).
The short-support mean value specializes the general mean value theorem (Lemma 3.1).
The $\mathcal{S}$-restricted theorem uses the completely multiplicative models $g_{\mathcal{J}}$ (Lemma 9.1).
The sieve estimate uses the lower-bound sieve weights (Lemma 10.3).
The sieve estimate uses the Euler-product upper bound (Lemma 10.5).

main_theorems.description: Short-interval theorems for complex-valued and non-vanishing multiplicative functions, with the sharp $\rho_1=1/3-2/(3\pi)$ range, and their quantitative and lower-bound forms.

definitions.description: The pretentious mean-value functional and the non-vanishing hypothesis.

general_and_smooth.description: The abstract multiplicative-subset result and its smooth-number consequences.

norm_forms.description: Applications to norm-forms of number fields in short intervals, and the number-field constants they rest on.

mean_and_halasz.description: The mean value theorems and Halász–Montgomery large-value inequalities for integer- and prime-supported Dirichlet polynomials, with the sieve majorant set.

decomposition_moment.description: The Dirichlet-polynomial decomposition, the moment computation, and the completely multiplicative models.

parseval_and_S.description: The Parseval bound on sparse Dirichlet polynomials and the resulting theorem inside the factorization set $\mathcal{S}$.

sieve_lowerbound.description: The sieve estimates gluing $\mathcal{S}$ back to all integers, and the lower-bound proposition behind the dense theorem.

MR1.title: Multiplicative functions in short intervals (Matomäki–Radziwiłł I)

Halasz.title: G. Halász — mean values of multiplicative functions

MontgomeryLargeValues.title: Montgomery — mean and large value theorems for Dirichlet polynomials
